item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This anomaly seems tonally dissonant with the rest of the GAW anomalies and the anomaly itself is not particularly interesting. It just exists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last subject was black boxed for a specific reason based on the results of their test.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was I wrong when I heard that you shouldn't black-box D-class designations? Should the last test subject be given a number?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like that the D Class at the end basically got to not be a D Class anymore, or at least that's how I read it. I'm hoping not, anyway.

I think this fits really well with how GAW handles their stupid Misters parodies, in basically being an inversion of what its name implies as some kind of karmic justice. I really detested AWCY when it was introduced, but came around to it as some really good quality stuff was introduced. GAW is the same, and this is a good example of what made me change my mind. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From reading their critique, it's mostly a critique on the particular tropes within this article. Particularly the omnipotent vigilante bit.

I haven't read the article, so I can't really comment on whether I agree with it or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right but Petro is treating the article like tropes are bad things to be avoided. You can't avoid tropes. Everything is a trope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tropes can be bad, tropes can be good. It all depends on the person. And how the tropes themselves are handled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh oh, someone spent too long on TV Tropes and now thinks tropes are bad things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love that the last D class turned out not to be a sexual predator, and his name was cleared, yet he was only "transferred to a more appropriate project" not amnestized and released.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it is kind of weird that the last test is used as like evidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just going by this:

1. This includes indviduals who have no public records or evidence of sexual misconduct.

It can even catch people who got away with it or perfectly covered their tracks, meaning a) it has some kind of insight into special knowledge and b) it can dish out justice where the system has failed *tropetropetrope*.

The way the document describes the SCP, there's never any doubt in its abilities expressed by the Foundation. It's especially supported by the test log where the innocent D-Class is exonerated.

Even if it does have a high false positive rate, that wouldn't fix anything. The indiscriminate vigilante bad guy trope is just as cliche as the perfect vigilante wish-fulfillment trope, and all the other problems still exit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How do you know if it has perfect knowledge? There might be a significant amount of false positives.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has a lot of tropes swirling around on it. I don't mean to say that they're poorly combined, but that I don't think they're advisable at all. So, this is:
- A magical vigilante with perfect knowledge.
- Its solution to sex offenders is literally what they already try to do with sex offenders, except in this case it works perfectly, never hits an innocent, and presumably doesn't have long-term health outcomes. Forgive me if I don't find "just like the system, but magic and more extreme" to be a clever ironic comeuppance.
- It also gives them alternative pleasures that act as standard-issue cursed wish-granters/compulsion effects. Which can probably kill them, because execution is fine too, right? Also, I get the feeling that you mean these things are pleasurable but not fetishes/sexual, but the language there should be more distinct.
- All of this is bookended by a meme and an internet fad/movement.

So yeah, it's a bunch of bad tropes stitched together, amounting to "our justice system but with more extreme punishment and without the bad parts." I'm all for GAW tackling sex offenders, but this just feels like a wish fulfillment power fantasy. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If AWCY? made this, absolutely everyone who called would get a fetish for rotten oatmeal or something.

Funny you should make that comparison to AWCY?. In fact, AWCY did already make this, and we know it as SCP-1127-4.

Aside from that, the Mr. References do feel a bit tacked on and there's nothing in the article that I can look back on and say "Wowzers, that was totally out there!" It's just…a thing, with no real hook to it.
-1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess that's because the Foundation doesn't know the number of entities either. Is there a way the Foundation could find out? i don't think it would add much to this skip, but i feel like they would at least try

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The point of the third log is to show that it does have some sort of intelligence (it mistakenly connects to someone it shouldn't have because it recognizes the Foundation's testing). It probably isn't as human as most of the Misters Against Weed, but at least more than Mr Bernie Sanders

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The former.

It's described as "an anomalous telephone service". There's no way to tell if it's one entity, multiple, or none: an answering machine, whatever they use for robocalls, you name it. Nothing about this screams "person".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is your issue the lack of a body or the lack of a personality?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mr. Deadly Sins and Mr. Ominous both set a precedent for ambiguously or explicitly non-embodied Misters Against Weed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like, being a person? An entity? Mr. Doggo is more of a Mister than this.

It really comes off as a perfectly valid phone-related anomaly that then has "Hey guys, it's a GAW Mister!" tacked on at the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is the hallmark of a Mister?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, it is in-character for members of GAW to not be very strict about what exactly qualifies as a "mister".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That works as a GAW skip, though. This just has zero hallmarks of being any kind of Mister.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ASMR is the scariest thing imaginable.

Who is John Roth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nicely done. Much better than what I probably would have pulled off.

Also like the touch with the last test, it's always nice to portray the Foundations sympathetic side.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you wanna look at it that way, it's no more GAW than SCP-3078 where the only literal reference to them was an out-of-universe tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It feels like the GAW addendum is just tacked on. I mean, literally nothing about this screams "Little Mister knockoff" until, surprise! it's there at the bottom for some reason.

Also, I got into a weird feedback loop after looking up ASMR, because the Wikipedia article reads like an SCP, and did not really satisfy my inquiry of "But is it real?" I mean, given that it's an internet fad, I guess that fits into GAW's M.O., but it still doesn't strike me as one of their Misters. Bizarre.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just thought he hit the space key twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gamers Against Weed have morals. If AWCY? made this, absolutely everyone who called would get a fetish for rotten oatmeal or something.

That said, GAW does actually have some in-universe roots in AWCY?, which some of the other SCPs and tales go into more detail about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They're commenting on your usage of

 [[==]] [[/==]]

in the article.

Personally, I don't mind it too much, but it does make the formatting look particularly gross on mobile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still see this whole GoI as a revival of AWCY? but trying to mask itself. Or maybe that's exactly what it is and I missed something.

I still like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure exactly what you mean

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Plaintext files don't have bolding." It's called hacking.

Seriously, though, I'll change the file type.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nitpick: Plaintext files don't have bolding. Otherwise good.

Also, just so everyone knows, because Monkeysky is a coward baby, the original draft of this had apples instead of oranges. The D-Class got a wicked bad tummyache.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's with the damn spacing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you to everyone who gave feedback on this, particularly Penton, kinchtheknifeblade, Decibelles, DolphinSlugchugger and Communism will win. If you helped me and you're not on the list, it's most likely because I don't know your username.
